_meta.draft_status: initial draft by Claude Code, Thu Apr 23 2026 — REQUIRES Barbara's curation. Spec §8 is explicit: the library is curated by Barbara, not generated by an agent. The agent's job is selection and sequencing, not discovery.
notes_for_barbara: I have NOT generated specific URLs. Khan Academy video IDs, Illustrative Mathematics task numbers, and YouTube URLs all rot and I can't verify them. What I've provided is: resource CATEGORIES with known source sites, modalities, descriptions of what they teach, and which misconceptions they target. You fill in the specific URLs during curation.
Target count is 15-25 per spec §8. I've drafted 20 skeleton entries: 2-3 per misconception, spanning modalities (video / manipulative / worksheet / game).
Per spec: 'fewer, better, more actionable' — prefer quality over count.
Montessori materials are physical — URLs don't apply; the description points to the material name and where to get it.
The `source_site` field holds the top-level site (e.g., khanacademy.org) so you can quickly verify each curation decision is in a trusted source.
When you curate: delete resources you don't like, add ones you do, replace descriptions that don't match how your setting uses them.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Equivalent fractions
resources.notes: Khan's equivalent-fractions intro uses a visual area model, which addresses the 'they look different' intuition directly.

resources.title: Fraction tiles / fraction bars — hands-on manipulative
resources.source_site: physical material — Lakeshore, Didax, or equivalent
resources.notes: Physical fraction bars (halves, thirds, fourths, sixths, eighths, twelfths all the same overall length). The learner can lay 2/4 on top of 1/2 and see they match. Excellent for m01 because it makes piece-size explicit: 1/8 piece is visibly smaller than 1/4 piece. Companion video: 'Explore Equivalent Fractions Using Fraction Bars' (Teach Elementary Math).

resources.title: Illustrative Mathematics — Halves, thirds, and sixths (3.NF.A.1)
resources.notes: Partitions a 6-square-unit rectangle into halves, thirds, and sixths. Surfaces equivalence (3/6 = 1/2). Hits m05 and m04. CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

resources.title: Math Antics — Fractions Are Parts (video)
resources.notes: Math Antics tends to be the video style that engages kids who dislike Khan. Use as an alternative when a kid rejects Khan for tone reasons.

resources.title: Paper folding for equivalent fractions — hands-on activity
resources.source_site: teacher-led activity — no URL, description follows
resources.notes: Fold a strip of paper in half, mark 1/2. Unfold. Fold again (now into fourths); see that the original 1/2 mark now aligns with 2/4. Repeat: fold again for eighths; same crease is at 4/8. Directly shows that the multiplicative doubling of denominator pairs with doubling of numerator — confronts the additive-equivalence misconception visually. Companion guide: Down River Resources article 'Top 4 Ways to Use Paper Folding to Teach Fractions Conceptually'.

resources.title: GeoGebra — Fractions on a number line
resources.notes: Sliders create proper and improper fractions on a number line; supports mixed-number conversion (e.g., 11/3, 21/8). Directly addresses m03 (fractions > 1 exist) and m01 (more pieces = smaller pieces).

resources.title: Khan Academy — Compare fractions with different denominators
resources.notes: Khan's comparison video explicitly addresses the 'bigger denominator' misconception using area-model visuals.

resources.title: Two-pizza comparison — real-world discussion activity
resources.notes: Cut two paper 'pizzas' of visibly different sizes. Each into fourths. Ask: 'Is 1/4 of this pizza the same as 1/4 of that pizza?' Make the size difference obvious. The referent-whole insight lands when the kid says the words 'it depends on the pizza size' in their own voice. Companion: Illustrative Mathematics task 819 'Comparing two different pizzas'.

resources.title: Open Up Resources K-5 Math — Grade 4 Unit 2: Fraction Equivalence and Comparison
resources.notes: Full lessons sequenced from like to unlike denominators. Section A uses fraction strips; Section B moves to number-line equivalence. Free public access.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Add fractions with common denominators
resources.notes: Direct instruction on why you add numerators but not denominators.

resources.title: Montessori fraction circles / fraction insets — physical manipulative
resources.source_site: Montessori material — Nienhuis, Alison's Montessori, Ikon, or similar Montessori supplier
resources.notes: Classic Montessori circular fraction insets: whole, halves, thirds, fourths, fifths, sixths, eighths, ninths, tenths. Kids can physically swap 2/4 pieces for 1/2 piece and confirm equivalence by matching perimeter. Strong fit for mastery-based settings. Companion video: 'Montessori Fraction Circles - The First Lesson'.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Fractions on the number line
resources.notes: Number line representation grounds fractions as single quantities.

resources.title: Chef's measuring cup challenge — kitchen activity
resources.notes: Measuring cups come in 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4. Give a recipe asking for 3/4 cup with only a 1/4 cup. Kid discovers 3 × 1/4 = 3/4. Scale up to '2/3 cup using 1/6 scoop' to build fluency. Applied mastery is the key test per spec §1. Companion: math4fun.io 'Kitchen Math for Kids' explainer.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Mixed numbers and improper fractions
resources.notes: Addresses m03 with explicit examples of fractions > 1.

resources.title: GeoGebra — Comparing Fractions (sliders)
resources.notes: Top sliders set numerator/denominator; bottom sliders multiply to reach equivalence. Supports comparison via equivalence reasoning.

resources.title: Math = Love — Fraction War printable card deck
resources.notes: Like the card game 'war' but with fraction cards. Each player flips a card; bigger fraction takes both. Builds comparison fluency with repeated reps. Free PDF, 24 cards 1/12 through 1; consider downloading and hosting locally to avoid link rot.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Add fractions with unlike denominators
resources.notes: Introduces common denominators using area models.

resources.title: Cuisenaire rods — fraction sense builder
resources.source_site: physical material — Didax, ETA, or similar
resources.notes: Cuisenaire rods are the classic tool for referent-whole reasoning: if orange (10 cm) is 'the whole', what is white (1 cm)? 1/10. But if red (2 cm) is 'the whole', what is white? 1/2. Directly confronts m06 (the whole doesn't matter). Companion: Annenberg Learner 'Fractions with Cuisenaire Rods' (45-min video session).

resources.title: Draw-your-own fraction picture — explain-your-thinking task
resources.notes: Give the kid a blank sheet. Prompt: 'Draw a picture that shows what 2/3 means. Then draw a picture that shows why 2/3 and 4/6 are the same.' The act of drawing surfaces misconceptions that typed-answer problems can hide. Useful as a guide-administered probe between assessments. Companion: YouCubed 'Fractions with Sense Making' (Cathy Humphreys).

resources.title: PhET — Fractions: Intro (number-line representation)
resources.notes: PhET's free sim lets the learner switch between bar, circle, and number-line views of the same fraction. The number-line view is the key one for grade 3 — it ties the size of a fraction to a single point, which is exactly what the CCSS 3.NF.A.2 number-line standard asks for. Walking from bars to the line in the same applet is a small concrete-to-abstract bridge. Research basis: IES practice guide 'Developing Effective Fractions Instruction K–8' (Recommendation 2: fractions are numbers; use number lines).

resources.title: Toy Theater — Number Line (drag-and-drop, configurable ticks)
resources.notes: Free, no-login web tool. Learner drags fraction labels onto a 0-to-1 line. Short, low-friction practice — works well as a warm-up before a longer task. Surfaces the whole-number bias immediately: a kid who thinks 1/8 > 1/4 will drop them in the wrong order on the line and see they don't fit. Research basis: Cramer et al. 2017 (Teaching Children Mathematics) on third-graders building number-line fluency.

resources.title: Fraction number-line floor mat — walk-the-line manipulative
resources.source_site: physical material — Didax, Learning Resources, or teacher-made painter's-tape line on the floor
resources.notes: A 0-to-1 (or 0-to-2) number line laid out on the floor. The learner physically walks to where a fraction lives ('stand where 3/4 is'). The whole-body movement makes 'distance from 0' real in a way a worksheet can't. The same line re-marked into halves, then thirds, then fourths makes equivalence (2/4 = 1/2) visible as the same spot. Cheap version: painter's tape on classroom floor with index-card landmarks. Research basis: Rodrigues et al. 2024 (Intervention in School and Clinic) on number-line fraction-magnitude instruction.

resources.title: GeoGebra — Plotting Fractions on a Number Line
resources.notes: Slider-driven number line where the learner sets the denominator and watches the tick marks redistribute between 0 and 1. The point is to see that the same 1/2 lives at the same spot, no matter whether the line is cut into 2s, 4s, 6s, or 8s — that's equivalence made spatial. Pairs well with r02 (fraction bars) as a representation-bridge activity. Note: GeoGebra has many community applets for this — verify the specific one before approving.

resources.title: 'Where does it live?' — guide-led number-line estimation probe
resources.notes: Quick diagnostic probe, not a lesson. Show a blank 0-to-1 number line. Ask the learner to point to where 1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/3, and 5/8 live. The pattern of errors tells the guide which misconception is active: putting 1/8 farther right than 1/4 → m01 (whole-number bias); putting 5/8 past 1 → m03 (fractions less than one not yet stable). Run as a 10-minute re-probe after a plan-tile is marked working. Research basis: Yuan & Chen 2023 (whole-number bias in fourth-grade number-line tasks).

resources.title: 'Which is bigger?' rapid card sort — whole-number-bias detector
resources.source_site: teacher/guide-led, no URL — index cards or printed cards
resources.notes: 5-minute diagnostic. Make 10–12 cards each showing a pair of fractions: 1/3 vs 1/4, 1/8 vs 1/2, 2/5 vs 2/7, 3/4 vs 3/8, etc. The learner sorts each card into 'left is bigger' or 'right is bigger' as fast as they can. The pattern of errors is the diagnosis: if she consistently picks the one with the bigger denominator, m01 (whole-number bias) is active. If she hesitates only on same-numerator pairs (3/4 vs 3/8), m01 is partially resolved — she's reasoning about pieces but not yet about piece size. Run before AND after a number-line lesson to measure shift. Research basis: Cramer et al. 2017 on third-grade fraction comparison errors.

resources.title: 'Closer to 0, 1/2, or 1?' benchmarking sort
resources.source_site: teacher/guide-led, no URL — printable or 3 hoops on the floor
resources.notes: Three labeled bins: 'closer to 0', 'closer to 1/2', 'closer to 1'. Hand the learner cards: 1/8, 2/3, 7/8, 4/9, 5/6, 1/12, 11/12, 3/8, 5/8, 4/7. She drops each into a bin and explains. This is a magnitude-estimation probe — the gold-standard task in the IES practice guide for diagnosing whether a kid has built a 'sense' of fraction size, not just procedural rules. Wrong placements that lump everything into one bin → m03 (no felt difference between fractions). Wrong placements driven by denominator size → m01.

resources.title: 'Same spot or different spot?' equivalence number-line probe
resources.notes: Show a 0-to-1 number line. For each pair, ask: 'do these live at the same spot or different spots?' Pairs to test: (1/2 and 2/4 → same), (2/3 and 4/6 → same), (1/3 and 1/4 → different), (3/4 and 6/8 → same), (1/2 and 2/3 → different — common m08 trap, since 1+1=2 and 2+1=3). The learner who marks (1/2, 2/3) as 'same' is showing m08 — building equivalence by adding instead of multiplying. Quick to run, very high diagnostic signal. Research basis: Behr/Lesh/Post on rational-number reasoning, plus IES practice guide Recommendation 2 (use number lines to teach equivalence).
